questions.question: Define proposition and give two examples of propositions and two examples of non-propositions.
keyPoints: Definition: A proposition is a declarative statement that is either true or false but not both
Example of proposition: 'Delhi is the capital of India' (true)
Example of proposition: '7 is a prime number' (true)
Non-proposition: 'What is your name?' (question)
Non-proposition: 'Close the door' (command)

questions.question: Construct the truth table for the compound proposition: (p ‚àß q) ‚Üí r
keyPoints: List all 8 combinations for 3 variables (p,q,r)
Calculate p‚àßq for each row
Then evaluate implication: (p‚àßq)‚Üír
Show that only fails when (p‚àßq)=T and r=F
Final column: T,T,T,T,T,T,F,T

questions.question: Verify whether (p ‚Üí q) ‚â° (¬¨p ‚à® q) using a truth table.
keyPoints: Construct truth table with columns for p, q, p‚Üíq, ¬¨p, ¬¨p‚à®q
Show all 4 combinations of p and q
Calculate p‚Üíq using implication rule
Calculate ¬¨p‚à®q using disjunction rule
Verify both columns match - they are logically equivalent

questions.question: Explain the difference between tautology, contradiction, and contingency with one example each.
keyPoints: Tautology: Always true - example: p ‚à® ¬¨p (law of excluded middle)
Contradiction: Always false - example: p ‚àß ¬¨p (law of non-contradiction)
Contingency: Sometimes true, sometimes false - example: p ‚Üí q depends on values
Explain that contingency depends on truth values of component propositions

questions.question: State and verify De Morgan's Law for negation of conjunction: ¬¨(p ‚àß q) ‚â° ¬¨p ‚à® ¬¨q
keyPoints: State the law clearly
Construct truth table with columns for p, q, p‚àßq, ¬¨(p‚àßq), ¬¨p, ¬¨q, ¬¨p‚à®¬¨q
Fill all 4 rows
Compare ¬¨(p‚àßq) and ¬¨p‚à®¬¨q columns
Both columns match - law verified

questions.question: Find the converse, inverse, and contrapositive of: 'If it rains, then the ground gets wet'.
keyPoints: Original: p ‚Üí q where p='it rains', q='ground gets wet'
Converse: q ‚Üí p = 'If ground gets wet, then it rains'
Inverse: ¬¨p ‚Üí ¬¨q = 'If it does not rain, then ground does not get wet'
Contrapositive: ¬¨q ‚Üí ¬¨p = 'If ground does not get wet, then it does not rain'
Note: Contrapositive is logically equivalent to original

questions.question: Using truth table method, prove that (p ‚Üí q) ‚àß (q ‚Üí r) ‚Üí (p ‚Üí r) is a tautology.
keyPoints: Construct table with variables p, q, r
Calculate p‚Üíq, q‚Üír for each row
Calculate (p‚Üíq) ‚àß (q‚Üír)
Calculate p‚Üír
Calculate final: (p‚Üíq)‚àß(q‚Üír) ‚Üí (p‚Üír)
Show final column is all T's - tautology proved

questions.question: Construct a complete truth table for the compound proposition: (p ‚à® q) ‚àß ¬¨(p ‚àß q). Determine if it is a tautology, contradiction, or contingency. Also identify what logical operation this represents.
keyPoints: Create table with columns: p, q, p‚à®q, p‚àßq, ¬¨(p‚àßq), (p‚à®q)‚àß¬¨(p‚àßq)
Fill 4 rows systematically
Show final column values: F, T, T, F
Identify as contingency (neither always T nor always F)
Recognize this is XOR (exclusive or) operation
XOR is true when exactly one operand is true

questions.question: Prove the following logical equivalences using truth tables: (a) p ‚Üî q ‚â° (p ‚Üí q) ‚àß (q ‚Üí p), (b) p ‚Üí (q ‚Üí r) ‚â° (p ‚àß q) ‚Üí r
keyPoints: For (a): Create columns for p, q, p‚Üîq, p‚Üíq, q‚Üíp, (p‚Üíq)‚àß(q‚Üíp)
Show both columns match for all 4 rows
For (b): Create columns for p, q, r, p‚Üí(q‚Üír), p‚àßq, (p‚àßq)‚Üír
Show both columns match for all 8 rows
Conclude both are logical equivalences

questions.question: Write the contrapositive of the following statements and verify that the contrapositive is logically equivalent to the original statement: (i) If x is even, then x¬≤ is even (ii) If a triangle has three equal sides, then it is equilateral
keyPoints: For (i): Original: p‚Üíq where p='x is even', q='x¬≤ is even'
Contrapositive: ¬¨q ‚Üí ¬¨p = 'If x¬≤ is odd, then x is odd'
Verify using truth table or logical reasoning
For (ii): Original: p‚Üíq where p='triangle has 3 equal sides', q='triangle is equilateral'
Contrapositive: ¬¨q ‚Üí ¬¨p = 'If triangle is not equilateral, then it does not have 3 equal sides'
Explain that contrapositive is always logically equivalent to original

questions.question: A compound proposition is defined as: [p ‚àß (q ‚à® r)] ‚Üî [(p ‚àß q) ‚à® (p ‚àß r)]. (a) Construct a complete truth table (b) Is this a tautology? (c) What law does this demonstrate?
keyPoints: (a) Create table with variables p, q, r and all intermediate steps
Need columns: q‚à®r, p‚àß(q‚à®r), p‚àßq, p‚àßr, (p‚àßq)‚à®(p‚àßr), final expression
Fill all 8 rows systematically
(b) Final column shows all T values - YES it is a tautology
(c) This demonstrates the Distributive Law: ‚àß distributes over ‚à®
State: p ‚àß (q ‚à® r) ‚â° (p ‚àß q) ‚à® (p ‚àß r)
